message.content:
## Source Extractor

Research question: "BEST LOCAL / OPEN-SOURCE TEXT-TO-SPEECH (TTS) MODELS TO REPLACE OR AUGMENT KOKORO for an English educational-podcast pipeline, as of 2026. Baseline to beat: Kokoro-82M (hexgrad/Kokoro-82M), currently run locally on Apple Silicon (CPU + MPS), producing 24kHz audio, multiple voices (af_heart/am_onyx/bf_isabella/bm_george etc.), American + British English, two-voice format (narrator + question voice). Evaluate candidates across these angles: (1) QUALITY/NATURALNESS — which open/local TTS models beat Kokoro on naturalness and intelligibility for English (candidates to assess: XTTS-v2/Coqui, Piper, StyleTTS2, F5-TTS, Fish-Speech/OpenAudio, Parler-TTS, MeloTTS, Orpheus-TTS, Chatterbox, Kitten-TTS, Zonos, and any newer 2025-2026 open models) — report MOS/quality benchmarks, license (commercial/redistribution OK?), and model size. (2) WORD/PHONEME-LEVEL TIMING & ALIGNMENT — which models natively output word/phoneme timestamps (durations), and what forced-alignment tools (Montreal Forced Aligner, WhisperX, aeneas, ctc-forced-aligner) best produce a text↔audio timing map from existing audio — this enables content-aware speed compression, script-highlighting, and a true text-to-audio map. (3) HIGH-SPEED COMPREHENSIBILITY — which model's output stays most intelligible under aggressive time-compression / at high playback speed (crisp consonants, clean formant transitions); also the best FORMANT/robotic option (eSpeak-NG, RHVoice, DECtalk-like) for an extreme 16x 'ultra' track. (4) MULTI-VOICE & VOICE CONTROL — voice variety, voice cloning, prosody/rate control, and ease of adding new voices for a narrator+question two-speaker format. (5) LOCAL-INFERENCE PRACTICALITY on Apple Silicon and CPU — real-time factor / batch render speed, RAM/VRAM needs, MPS/CoreML/ONNX support, latency, and integration effort to swap into a Python pipeline that today calls Kokoro. For EACH recommended model give: quality vs Kokoro, license, local-inference feasibility on a Mac, whether it emits alignment, and a clear verdict on whether it's worth switching to (or adding alongside Kokoro). Prefer authoritative/benchmark sources and recent (2025-2026) comparisons; flag where claims are vendor marketing vs independent evaluation."

Fetch and extract key claims from this source:
**URL:** https://www.mangolassi.org/covell/1997-061/index.html
**Title:** Mach1: Nonuniform Time-Scale Modification of Speech (Covell, Withgott, Slaney — Interval Research)
**Found via:** specialized — high-speed intelligibility & robotic formant track search

## Task
1. Use WebFetch to retrieve the page content.
2. Assess source quality: primary research/institution? secondary reporting? blog/opinion? forum? unreliable?
3. Extract 2-5 FALSIFIABLE claims that bear on the research question. Each claim must:
   - be a concrete, checkable statement (not vague generalities)
   - include a direct quote from the source as support
   - be rated central/supporting/tangential to the research question
4. Note publish date if available.

If the fetch fails or the page is irrelevant/paywalled, return claims: [] and sourceQuality: "unreliable".

Structured output only.

Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.

Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.

- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.

content.text: I'll fetch the source content first.

input.prompt: Extract the key claims about nonuniform time-scale modification of speech (Mach1). Focus on: (1) high-speed comprehensibility and time-compression of speech, (2) intelligibility of compressed speech, (3) methods like SOLA/synchronized overlap-add, (4) any measured results comparing nonuniform vs uniform/linear time compression, listener comprehension scores, or compression ratios. Provide exact quotes for each claim. Also note the publication date, authors, and institution.

content.content:
# Mach1: Key Claims on Nonuniform Time-Scale Speech Modification

**Publication Details:**
- Authors: Michele Covell, Margaret Withgott, Malcolm Slaney
- Institution: Interval Research Corporation
- Date: 1997 (Technical Report IRC-TR 1997-061); Conference presentation May 1998

---

## High-Speed Comprehensibility & Compression Rates

The research achieved significantly higher usable compression rates than prior work:

> "The resulting compressed speech remains comprehensible at much higher rates: as high as 2.56 to 4.15 times real time and 390 to 673 wpm."

> "At compression rates between 2.5 and 4.2 times real time, there was no significant comprehension loss with increasing Mach1 compression rates."

---

## Intelligibility: Mach1 vs. Linear Compression

Measured comprehension improvements were substantial:

> "At identical overall compression rates, listener comprehension for Mach1-compressed speech increased between 5 and 31 percentage points over that for linearly compressed speech, and response times dropped by 15%."

More detailed breakdown:

> "At compression rates between 2.5 and 4.2 times real time, comprehension of Mach1-compressed speech is 17 percentage points better than that of linearly compressed speech."

Preference testing showed strong listener preference:

> "In A-B preference tests, Mach1-compressed speech was chosen 95% of the time."

---

## Technical Methods

The document does not discuss SOLA or synchronized overlap-add techniques.

content.text: The fetch succeeded. Let me get more detail on the technical methods used.

content.content:
# Mach1 Technical Method and Experimental Setup

Based on the provided webpage, I cannot give you the specific technical details you're requesting.

## What's Available

The page states that technical methodology is described in "Section 3 of our technical report" but **does not explain the actual algorithm or decision-making process on this page**. It doesn't specify whether compression decisions are based on emphasis, pauses, prosody, or other acoustic features.

## What's Missing

The webpage lacks:
- Explanation of how Mach1 determines compression rates at different speech segments
- Details about listener counts or experimental methodology
- Audio source specifications beyond "Kaplan TOEFL review materials"

## Recommendation

To answer your questions thoroughly, you would need to consult the full technical report (available in the HTML, PostScript, or PDF versions linked at the bottom of the page), as the summary page focuses on results rather than methodology.

The abstract only mentions that results were measured and preference tests conducted, but provides no experimental protocol details on this particular webpage.

claims.claim: At identical overall compression rates, listener comprehension of nonuniformly (Mach1) compressed speech is 5 to 31 percentage points higher than linearly/uniformly compressed speech, with 15% faster response times.

input.publishDate: 1997 (Interval Research Technical Report IRC-TR 1997-061; presented May 1998)

content.text:
Source extracted successfully. Summary of what I found at https://www.mangolassi.org/covell/1997-061/index.html

This is a primary research technical report (Interval Research Corporation Technical Report IRC-TR 1997-061, 1997, presented May 1998) by Covell, Withgott, and Slaney on "Mach1: Nonuniform Time-Scale Modification of Speech." It is directly relevant to angle (3) of the research question — high-speed comprehensibility under aggressive time compression.

Key falsifiable findings (all with direct quotes):
- Nonuniform time-scaling keeps speech comprehensible up to 2.56-4.15x real time (390-673 wpm).
- Nonuniform (Mach1) compression beats linear/uniform compression by 5-31 percentage points in comprehension at equal compression rates, with 15% faster response times.
- About 17 percentage-point comprehension advantage in the 2.5-4.2x range, with no significant comprehension loss as Mach1's rate increases.
- 95% listener preference for Mach1 over linear compression in A-B tests.

Caveat: the summary page does not disclose the actual algorithm (how it allocates compression across segments) or the experimental protocol (listener counts) — those are deferred to Section 3 of the full technical report. The audio source was Kaplan TOEFL review materials.

Note this is a methods/perceptual finding about time compression itself (relevant to the "high-speed comprehensibility / ultra track" sub-question), not a comparison of any specific local TTS model versus Kokoro.
